name: MLForecast (Nixtla Scalable ML for Time Series)

performance.notes: Highly optimized; Rust-backed feature computation; scales to millions of series

agent_readiness.notes: ML-based time series forecasting at scale using sklearn-compatible models. from mlforecast import MLForecast. from mlforecast.lag_transforms import RollingMean. fcst = MLForecast(models=[LGBMRegressor()], freq='D', lags=[1, 7, 14], lag_transforms={1: [RollingMean(7)]}). fcst.fit(df). fcst.predict(h=14). Data format: long format with unique_id, ds, y columns. Distributed: with Spark, Ray, or Dask. Rust feature engine (coreforecast). Part of Nixtla ecosystem with StatsForecast and NeuralForecast. Cross-validation: fcst.cross_validation(df, h=14, n_windows=3).

gotchas: Data must be in long format (unique_id, ds, y) — reshaping required from wide format
Requires understanding of lag feature engineering for effective use
Model-specific HPO must be done separately
coreforecast Rust dependency may complicate some environments
best_when: Millions of time series needing ML-based forecasting at scale
Retail demand forecasting, financial forecasting with ML models
When LightGBM/XGBoost models outperform statistical baselines
avoid_when: Single time series (use prophet or statsmodels)
When interpretable statistical models are required (use StatsForecast)
When no ML engineering for lag features is available
summary: MLForecast scales ML models (LightGBM, XGBoost, sklearn) for time series forecasting across millions of series. MLForecast(models=[LGBMRegressor()], freq='D', lags=[1,7,14]).fit(df).predict(h=14). Long format data (unique_id, ds, y). Rust-backed feature computation (coreforecast). Distributed via Spark/Ray/Dask. Cross-validation built in. Apache-2.0. Part of Nixtla ecosystem alongside StatsForecast and NeuralForecast. Best for retail/financial large-scale ML forecasting.
